docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Database Systems Engineering Master databases by building one.

SreeBase is an educational, pure-Python document database with a bracketless query language. Learn how storage engines, custom parsers, and secondary indexes work under the hood without the enterprise bloat.

Start Learning View Syntax Code as Data

Forget brackets and semicolons. SreeBase uses an elegant indentation-based query language inspired by Python, making database interactions incredibly clean.

insert into employees
    name = "Anika"
    role = "Engineering"
    salary = 90000
Under the Hood

Learn core concepts like append-only Bitcask storage engines, in-memory hash indexes, custom lexing/parsing, and binary TCP protocols.

get employees
    role = "Engineering"
    sort by salary desc
    limit 5

docs.title: Learning Tracks1. Core Concepts2. Query Mastery3. Build & Deploy4. Resources
docs.text:
• Installation & Setup Get SreeBase running on your machine
  • Your First Database Create collections and insert records

  • Aggregations Sum, Count, Avg operators
  • Using Indexes O(1) lookups for fast queries
  • Python SDK Integrate SreeBase into your apps
  • Example Project Build a full app with ReddyBase
  • Security & Admin Roles, users, and RBAC
  • FAQ & Common Pitfalls Indentation, blank lines, & modes
  • Future Roadmap Joins, transactions, and beyond
  • Cheat Sheet Quick syntax reference

docs.title: About the Author
docs.text:
Hello World! 👋

Welcome to SreeBase, an educational database engineering project built from the ground up!

My name is Gowtham Reddy, and I am a software engineer passionate about systems design, backend architecture, and understanding how things work under the hood.

docs.title: Why I Built SreeBase
docs.text:
Database systems often feel like magic. You write a SQL or NoSQL query, and gigabytes of data are sorted, filtered, and returned in milliseconds.

I built SreeBase to demystify that magic. I wanted to understand:

  1. How do parsers work? SreeBase has a custom, hand-written Lexer and Parser for its unique bracketless query language.
  2. How does disk I/O work? SreeBase uses a Bitcask-style storage engine (similar to Riak) with an append-only log and background compaction.
  3. How do drivers connect? SreeBase features a custom TCP protocol with a length-prefixed binary header, and a native Python SDK (reddybase).

docs.title: Connect With Me
docs.text:
If you are interested in backend engineering, database internals, or just want to chat about Python, feel free to connect!

  • 🐙 GitHub: gowthamrdyy
  • 💼 Project Repo: SreeBase

"The best way to understand complex systems is to build a smaller version of them yourself."

docs.title: Python SDK: reddybase
docs.text: The reddybase package is the Python client for talking to a running SreeBase TCP server.

docs.title: Connect
docs.text:
from reddybase.client.driver import Client

client = Client(host="127.0.0.1", port=6969)
client.login("admin", "supersecret")

Use a context manager when possible:

from reddybase.client.driver import Client

with Client() as client:
    client.login("admin", "supersecret")
    users = client.collection("users")
    print(users.get())

docs.title: Collections
docs.text:
users = client.collection("users")

Collection names must be simple identifiers such as users, server_logs, or app.events.

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
users.insert({
    "name": "Maya",
    "role": "developer",
    "salary": 120000,
    "active": True,
})

Supported SDK literal values:

  • str
  • int
  • finite float
  • bool
  • None

Other Python objects are rejected so they cannot accidentally become unsafe query text.

docs.title: Get
docs.text:
Equality filters use normal values:

developers = users.get(where={"role": "developer"})

Comparisons use tuple conditions:

senior_developers = users.get(
    where={
        "role": "developer",
        "salary": (">=", 100000),
    },
    sort=("salary", "desc"),
    limit=10,
)

Use tuple comparisons

Do not write operators inside strings. Use (">", 10), (">=", 10), ("!=", "guest"), and similar tuples.

docs.title: Update
docs.text:
users.update(
    where={"role": "developer"},
    set_fields={"active": True}
)

docs.title: Delete
docs.text:
users.delete(where={"active": False})

Warning

Calling users.delete() without a filter deletes every document in the collection.

docs.title: Aggregate
docs.text:
stats = users.aggregate(
    group_by="role",
    calculate=["avg(salary)", "count()"],
    where={"active": True}
)

docs.title: Raw Queries
docs.text:
For advanced usage, you can send query text directly:

result = client.raw_query("""
get users
    role = "developer"
""")

Use the collection methods when taking input from users. They validate identifiers and escape string values.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from reddybase.client.driver import Client, ReddyBaseError

try:
    with Client() as client:
        client.login("admin", "wrong-password")
except ReddyBaseError as exc:
    print(f"SreeBase error: {exc}")

docs.title: FAQ & Common Pitfalls
docs.text: Welcome to SreeBase! Because SreeBase features a unique bracketless query language, first-time users often run into a couple of common syntax pitfalls.

docs.title: 1. Why does my query show ... and not run when I press Enter?
docs.text:
SreeBase queries are multi-line. Since there are no curly braces {} or semicolons ; that hard-terminate a query block, the shell does not automatically know when you have finished typing.

When you type a query and press Enter, the shell prompts you with ... to allow you to add more filters, assignments, or limits on the next line.

How to fix it: To execute the query, simply press Enter twice at the end of your query (i.e., submit an empty line).

sreebase> get cast
      ...                     <-- Press Enter on this blank line!

docs.title: 2. I get a [ParserError] Expected indented assignments when inserting
docs.text:
This happens because SreeBase relies entirely on indentation to understand which block a line belongs to (exactly like Python).

The ... prompt is just a visual indicator; it does not add physical spaces to your query.

Incorrect (0 indentation): 

sreebase> insert into users
      ... name="gowtham"      <-- Error: Expected indentation

Correct (Indented with Spaces): You must press the Spacebar (e.g., 4 spaces) before typing the assignment. 

sreebase> insert into users
      ...     name="gowtham"  <-- Correct!

docs.title: 3. Should I use sreebase serve or sreebase local?
docs.text:
• Use sreebase local for rapid prototyping, learning, and single-terminal usage. It functions exactly like SQLite, running the embedded database engine directly in your terminal.
  • Use sreebase serve when you are running an actual application (using the Python SDK) or you want multiple clients to connect to the database simultaneously over TCP. Remember, serve occupies the terminal, so you must use a second terminal window to run sreebase shell.

docs.title: 4. How do I quit the shell?
docs.text: Type exit, quit, \q, or press Ctrl+D to safely exit the interactive REPL.

docs.title: Future Roadmap & Advanced Features
docs.text:
SreeBase is continuously evolving. Our ultimate goal is to provide a bracketless, document-oriented database that still supports the powerful relational guarantees found in advanced databases like PostgreSQL.

Here is a look at the advanced features we plan to implement in the future:

docs.title: 1. Relational Joins
docs.text:
Currently, SreeBase is a pure document store where collections are isolated. We plan to introduce SQL-style JOIN syntax adapted for our bracketless language.

Planned Syntax Example: 

get orders
    join users on orders.user_id = users._id
    status = "shipped"
This will allow fetching embedded documents across collections without doing application-side joins.

docs.title: 2. ACID Transactions
docs.text:
A core feature of advanced SQL databases is the ability to guarantee atomic transactions. We plan to implement BEGIN, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK commands.

Planned Syntax Example: 

begin transaction
insert into accounts
    balance = -100
insert into accounts
    balance = +100
commit

docs.title: 3. Advanced Aggregations (GROUP BY & HAVING)
docs.text:
While SreeBase currently supports basic aggregations (sum, count, avg), we will expand this to support grouping by multiple fields and filtering post-aggregation using a having clause.

Planned Syntax Example: 

aggregate employees
    group by department
    select count() as num_employees
    having num_employees > 10

docs.title: 4. Foreign Keys and Constraints
docs.text: To ensure data integrity, we plan to allow schema validation and referential integrity directly at the storage engine level. - UNIQUE constraints (enforced by secondary indexes). - FOREIGN KEY constraints (preventing deletion of parent records).

docs.title: 5. Security Enhancements
docs.text:
• TLS/SSL Encryption: Wrapping the current raw TCP socket protocol in TLS to encrypt data over the wire.
  • Granular RBAC: Expanding roles beyond just admin and read to include collection-specific permissions (e.g., write:users).

docs.title: Bracketless Query Syntax
docs.text: SreeBase queries are written as plain text. Instead of braces and commas, blocks are created with indentation.

docs.title: Rules To Remember
docs.text:
• The first line starts the command.
  • Indented lines belong to that command.
  • A blank line in the CLI executes the query.
  • Strings use double quotes.
  • Supported values are strings, numbers, booleans, and null.
  • Conditions use =, !=, >, >=, <, or <=.

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
insert into users
    name = "Maya"
    role = "developer"
    age = 28
    active = true

docs.title: Get
docs.text:
get users
    role = "developer"
    age >= 21
    sort by age desc
    limit 10

docs.title: Update
docs.text:
update users
    where
        role = "developer"
    set
        active = true

docs.title: Delete
docs.text:
delete from users
    active = false

Delete without conditions

delete from users deletes every document in the users collection.

docs.title: Create Index
docs.text:
create index on users field role

Indexes speed up equality filters such as:

get users
    role = "developer"

docs.title: Show Collections
docs.text: show collections

docs.title: Aggregate
docs.text:
aggregate users
    where
        active = true
    group by role
    calculate avg(age), count()

docs.title: Users and Login
docs.text:
The first user can be created anonymously on a fresh database:

create user admin password "supersecret" role admin

After that, log in before running normal queries:

login admin password "supersecret"

docs.title: Supported Roles
docs.text: Role Can read normal collections Can write normal collections Can manage users Can access _system.* admin Yes Yes Yes Yes read Yes No No No

docs.title: SreeBase Tutorial Roadmap
docs.text: This tutorial is written for new learners. You do not need to know database internals before starting.

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text: Step Lesson Goal 1 Installation Run the server and CLI 2 First Database Insert and read your first documents 3 Core Concepts Understand collections, documents, indexes, and roles 4 Insert Data Add records safely 5 Read Data Filter, sort, and limit results 6 Update Data Change matching documents 7 Delete Data Remove documents carefully 8 Aggregations Group and calculate summaries 9 Indexes Speed up equality lookups 10 Python SDK Build an app with reddybase

docs.title: What SreeBase Teaches
docs.text:
• How a database stores records in files.
  • How a lexer and parser turn text into executable commands.
  • How indexes trade memory for faster reads.
  • How a TCP server can expose a database over the network.
  • Why security and validation matter around query languages.

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
You should have:

  • Python 3.11 or newer
  • A terminal
  • Basic comfort with Python dictionaries

docs.title: Aggregations
docs.text: Aggregations group documents and calculate summary values.

docs.title: Count By Group
docs.text:
aggregate users
    group by role
    calculate count()

docs.title: Average Salary By Role
docs.text:
aggregate employees
    where
        active = true
    group by role
    calculate avg(salary), count()

docs.title: Supported Functions
docs.text: Function Example Meaning count() count() number of documents in the group sum(field) sum(salary) sum numeric values avg(field) avg(salary) average numeric values

docs.title: Missing Or Non-Numeric Values
docs.text: For sum() and avg(), non-numeric values are ignored. If no numeric values exist for avg(), the result is 0.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
Group logs by status:

aggregate logs
    group by status
    calculate count()

docs.title: Server
docs.text:
sreebase serve
sreebase serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 6969 --data-dir ./data

docs.title: Shell
docs.text:
sreebase shell
sreebase shell -u admin

docs.title: Users
docs.text:
create user admin password "secret" role admin
login admin password "secret"

docs.title: Insert
docs.text:
insert into users
    name = "Maya"
    age = 28

docs.title: Get
docs.text:
get users
    age >= 18
    sort by age desc
    limit 10

docs.title: Update
docs.text:
update users
    where
        name = "Maya"
    set
        active = true

docs.title: Delete
docs.text:
delete from users
    name = "Maya"

docs.title: Index
docs.text: create index on users field name

docs.title: Aggregate
docs.text:
aggregate users
    group by role
    calculate count()

docs.title: SDK
docs.text:
from reddybase.client.driver import Client

with Client() as client:
    client.login("admin", "secret")
    users = client.collection("users")
    users.insert({"name": "Maya", "age": 28})
    users.get(where={"age": (">=", 18)}, sort=("age", "desc"))

docs.title: Database
docs.text:
In SreeBase, a database is a folder that stores collection files.

data/
  users.sree
  orders.sree
  _system.users.sree

docs.title: Collection
docs.text:
A collection is a named group of documents. It is similar to a table in SQL or a collection in MongoDB.

insert into users
    name = "Maya"

The collection name is users.

docs.title: Document
docs.text:
A document is a key-value object.

{
  "_id": "generated-id",
  "name": "Maya",
  "role": "developer"
}

SreeBase automatically adds _id if you do not provide one.

docs.title: Query
docs.text:
A query is text that tells SreeBase what to do.

get users
    role = "developer"

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
An index speeds up equality lookups for one field.

create index on users field role

After that, this query can use the index:

get users
    role = "developer"

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
SreeBase has two roles:

  • admin: can read, write, create users, and access system collections.
  • read: can read normal collections only.

docs.title: Storage Engine
docs.text:
SreeBase uses append-only files. Inserts, updates, and deletes append records instead of rewriting the whole file immediately.

Compaction can later rewrite the file to keep only live documents.

docs.title: Delete Data
docs.text: Use delete from to remove documents.

docs.title: Delete Matching Documents
docs.text:
delete from users
    active = false

docs.title: Delete One Document
docs.text:
delete from users
    _id = "user-001"

docs.title: Delete Everything
docs.text:
delete from users

Danger

Delete without conditions removes every document in the collection.

docs.title: How Delete Works
docs.text: SreeBase appends a tombstone record. During recovery, tombstones tell the storage engine that the document is no longer live.

docs.title: Example App With Python
docs.text: This example uses the SDK to create a small learning tracker.

docs.title: Start SreeBase
docs.text: sreebase serve

docs.title: Python Script
docs.text:
from reddybase.client.driver import Client, ReddyBaseError

def main():
    with Client(host="127.0.0.1", port=6969) as client:
        client.login("admin", "supersecret")

        courses = client.collection("courses")

        courses.insert({
            "title": "SreeBase Basics",
            "level": "beginner",
            "lessons": 12,
            "published": True,
        })

        courses.insert({
            "title": "Storage Engines",
            "level": "intermediate",
            "lessons": 8,
            "published": True,
        })

        beginner_courses = courses.get(
            where={"level": "beginner", "lessons": (">=", 5)},
            sort=("lessons", "desc"),
        )

        print(beginner_courses)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main()
    except ReddyBaseError as exc:
        print(f"SreeBase error: {exc}")

docs.title: What This Shows
docs.text:
• Connect to a running server.
  • Authenticate.
  • Select a collection.
  • Insert documents.
  • Query with safe tuple comparisons.
  • Sort results.

docs.title: Next Ideas
docs.text:
• Add a FastAPI route that reads courses.
  • Store user progress.
  • Add an index on level.

docs.title: Your First Database
docs.text: In this lesson you will create a collection, insert documents, and query them back.

docs.title: 1. Start the Server
docs.text: sreebase serve

docs.title: 2. Open the Shell
docs.text: sreebase shell -u admin

docs.title: 3. Insert Documents
docs.text:
insert into books
    title = "Database Systems"
    author = "Navya"
    pages = 420
    available = true

insert into books
    title = "Python Backends"
    author = "Arjun"
    pages = 260
    available = true

docs.title: 4. Read All Books
docs.text: get books

docs.title: 5. Filter Books
docs.text:
get books
    pages > 300

docs.title: 6. Sort and Limit
docs.text:
get books
    available = true
    sort by pages desc
    limit 1

docs.title: What Happened
docs.text:
• books became a collection.
  • Each insert created one document.
  • SreeBase stored those documents in books.sree.
  • get books scanned the live documents and returned matches.

docs.title: Indexes
docs.text: Indexes make equality lookups faster.

docs.title: Create An Index
docs.text: create index on users field role

docs.title: Query With An Indexed Field
docs.text:
get users
    role = "developer"

docs.title: Good Index Fields
docs.text:
Choose fields that appear often in equality filters:

  • username
  • email
  • role
  • status
  • department

docs.title: What Indexes Do Not Speed Up Yet
docs.text:
Current SreeBase indexes are simple hash maps. They do not speed up:

  • range filters such as age > 18
  • sorting
  • text search
  • compound conditions across multiple fields unless each equality field has its own index

docs.title: Index Persistence
docs.text: SreeBase stores index definitions in system metadata. When a collection is reopened, the index is rebuilt from the stored documents.

docs.title: Insert Data
docs.text: Use insert into to add a document to a collection.

docs.title: Basic Insert
docs.text:
insert into users
    name = "Maya"
    role = "developer"
    age = 28

docs.title: Supported Values
docs.text:
insert into examples
    title = "Hello"
    count = 10
    price = 19.99
    active = true
    deleted_at = null

docs.title: Custom _id
docs.text:
If you want to choose the document ID yourself:

insert into users
    _id = "user-001"
    name = "Maya"

If _id already exists, SreeBase raises a duplicate-key error.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
Create a courses collection:

insert into courses
    title = "SreeBase Basics"
    level = "beginner"
    lessons = 12

Then read it:

get courses

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page shows how to run SreeBase locally.

docs.title: Option 1: Install With Pip
docs.text:
pip install sreebase

Start the server:

sreebase serve

By default, the server listens on:

127.0.0.1:6969

docs.title: Option 2: Run From This Repository
docs.text:
From the project folder:

python -m sreebase serve

If your system uses python3:

python3 -m sreebase serve

docs.title: Start the CLI
docs.text:
Open a second terminal:

sreebase shell

On a fresh database, create your first admin user:

create user admin password "supersecret" role admin

After that, reconnect with a username:

sreebase shell -u admin

docs.title: Where Data Is Stored
docs.text:
By default, SreeBase writes files into:

./data

Use another directory:

sreebase serve --data-dir ./my_data

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
docker compose up --build

The compose file maps the server to port 6969 and stores data in a local volume folder.

docs.title: Read Data
docs.text: Use get to read documents from a collection.

docs.title: Read Everything
docs.text: get users

docs.title: Equality Filter
docs.text:
get users
    role = "developer"

docs.title: Comparison Filters
docs.text:
get users
    age >= 18

Available operators:

Operator Meaning = equal != not equal > greater than >= greater than or equal < less than <= less than or equal

docs.title: Multiple Conditions
docs.text:
Conditions are combined with AND logic.

get users
    role = "developer"
    active = true
    age >= 21

docs.title: Sort
docs.text:
get users
    sort by age asc

get users
    sort by age desc

docs.title: Limit
docs.text:
get users
    sort by age desc
    limit 5

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
Find the top 3 active developers by salary:

get employees
    role = "developer"
    active = true
    sort by salary desc
    limit 3

docs.title: Admin And Security
docs.text: SreeBase includes basic authentication and role-based access control.

docs.title: Bootstrap The First Admin
docs.text:
On a fresh database:

create user admin password "supersecret" role admin

This is allowed only when no users exist.

docs.title: Login
docs.text: login admin password "supersecret"

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Role Purpose admin Full access, including user creation and _system.* collections read Read-only access to normal collections

Create a read-only user:

create user analyst password "readonlypass" role read

docs.title: Password Storage
docs.text:
SreeBase stores passwords as salted PBKDF2-HMAC hashes. It does not store new passwords as plaintext.

Older plaintext user records are migrated after a successful login.

docs.title: System Collections
docs.text:
Internal collections start with _system..

Non-admin users cannot access them.

docs.title: Current Security Limits
docs.text:
SreeBase is still a learning project. It does not currently provide:

  • TLS for the TCP protocol
  • login rate limiting
  • account lockout
  • audit logs
  • encryption at rest
  • collection-level permission policies

Use it on localhost or trusted private networks unless you add those protections.

docs.title: Storage Engine
docs.text: SreeBase stores each collection in an append-only .sree file.

docs.title: File Per Collection
docs.text:
data/
  users.sree
  logs.sree
  _system.users.sree

docs.title: Append-Only Writes
docs.text:
When you insert or update a document, SreeBase appends a new record to the file.

When you delete a document, SreeBase appends a tombstone.

docs.title: In-Memory Index
docs.text:
At startup, SreeBase replays the file and builds an in-memory map:

document_id -> file offset

This lets point reads seek directly to the latest version of a document.

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
On restart, the engine reads records from the beginning of the file:

  • latest write wins
  • tombstones remove documents
  • torn trailing records are truncated

docs.title: Compaction
docs.text:
Compaction rewrites live documents into a new file and swaps it into place.

Current compaction uses an exclusive lock. That means reads and writes wait while compaction runs, but the behavior is simple and correct.

docs.title: Sync Policy
docs.text:
The storage engine supports:

  • EVERY_WRITE: fsync after every write
  • BATCH: fsync after a batch of writes or manual sync

docs.title: Server Will Not Start
docs.text:
Check whether port 6969 is already in use.

Try another port:

sreebase serve --port 6970

Then connect:

sreebase shell -p 6970

docs.title: Authentication Required
docs.text:
After the first user exists, anonymous queries are rejected.

Use:

sreebase shell -u admin

docs.title: Invalid Username Or Password
docs.text:
Check:

  • the username spelling
  • the password
  • whether the server is using the expected --data-dir

docs.title: Query Fails With Parser Error
docs.text:
Common causes:

  • missing newline
  • wrong indentation
  • missing quotes around strings
  • using unsupported values such as lists or objects

docs.title: Delete Removed Too Much Data
docs.text:
delete from collection deletes all documents in a collection.

Prefer filtered deletes:

delete from users
    _id = "user-001"

docs.title: SDK Comparison Does Not Work
docs.text:
Use tuple comparisons:

users.get(where={"age": (">=", 18)})

Do not place operators inside strings.

docs.title: Update Data
docs.text: Use update to modify matching documents.

docs.title: Update Matching Documents
docs.text:
update users
    where
        role = "developer"
    set
        active = true

docs.title: Update One Document By ID
docs.text:
update users
    where
        _id = "user-001"
    set
        role = "admin"

docs.title: Update All Documents
docs.text:
If you omit where, every document in the collection is updated.

update users
    set
        active = false

Warning

Update without where is powerful. Use it only when you really want to change every document.

docs.title: How Update Works
docs.text: SreeBase reads matching documents, merges the new fields, and appends updated versions to the storage file.
